title: Dog Open Sore on Paw Not Healing: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your dog's paw sore isn't healing, the urgency of veterinary care, and how clear photos help our triage team assess the wound.
content_html:
What Is a Non-Healing Paw Sore?

An open sore on a dog's paw that fails to close or improve over several days can represent various underlying issues. Common causes include lick granulomas, interdigital cysts, fungal or bacterial infections, foreign bodies like grass seeds lodged in the skin, or even certain types of skin tumors. Because dogs frequently lick their paws for comfort, they often introduce more bacteria and prevent the tissue from knitting back together.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is categorized as Medium. While a non-healing sore is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it requires veterinary attention within 24 to 48 hours. Delays can lead to deep-tissue infections, cellulitis, or the spread of a potentially malignant growth. If your dog is limping, lethargic, or the paw is significantly swollen and hot to the touch, you should seek care sooner.

How Photos Help Our Triage Team

Providing a high-quality, well-lit photograph of the lesion is incredibly beneficial for triage. Visual details allow veterinary professionals to assess the color, discharge, and borders of the sore. This helps us differentiate between a simple abrasion and a more complex growth, ensuring your pet is prioritized correctly and that we provide the most accurate advice before you arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter ointments on my dog's paw?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid human ointments like Neosporin unless directed by a vet, as many contain ingredients that can be toxic if your dog licks them off.

faqs.question: Why does my dog keep licking the sore?
faqs.answer: Dogs lick wounds due to instinct, but it often causes 'lick granulomas,' where the repetitive friction prevents healing and causes more inflammation.

faqs.question: Should I bandage the paw at home?
faqs.answer: Generally, it is safer to use an Elizabethan collar (the 'cone') to stop licking rather than bandaging, as improper bandaging can cut off circulation or trap moisture.
